content.text:
Congruences, introduced by Gauss, provide a powerful language for 
discussing divisibility.

content.title: Congruence
content.content:
Let $n$ be a positive integer. Two integers $a$ and $b$ are 
**congruent modulo $n$**, written $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$, if 
$n \mid (a - b)$.

Equivalently, $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ if $a$ and $b$ have the same 
remainder when divided by $n$.

content.title: Properties of Congruences
content.content:
Congruence modulo $n$ is an equivalence relation:

1. **Reflexive:** $a \equiv a \pmod{n}$.

2. **Symmetric:** If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$, then $b \equiv a \pmod{n}$.

3. **Transitive:** If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ and $b \equiv c \pmod{n}$, 
then $a \equiv c \pmod{n}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: Congruences can be added, subtracted, and multiplied like equations.

content.title: Congruence Arithmetic
content.content:
If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ and $c \equiv d \pmod{n}$, then:

1. $a + c \equiv b + d \pmod{n}$

2. $a - c \equiv b - d \pmod{n}$

3. $ac \equiv bd \pmod{n}$

4. $a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$ for all positive integers $k$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) We have $n \mid (a - b)$ and $n \mid (c - d)$. Therefore 
$n \mid [(a - b) + (c - d)] = (a + c) - (b + d)$.

(c) From $a \equiv b$ and $c \equiv d$, we have $ac \equiv bc \equiv bd$.

content.text:
**Warning:** Division is not always valid! From $6 \equiv 0 \pmod{6}$, 
we cannot conclude $3 \equiv 0 \pmod{6}$.

content.title: Residue Class
content.content:
The **residue class** of $a$ modulo $n$, denoted 
$[a]$ or $\bar{a}$, is the set of all integers congruent to $a$ modulo $n$:
\[
[a] = \{a + kn : k \in \mathbb{Z}\}.
\]

content.text:
There are exactly $n$ distinct residue classes modulo $n$, represented by 
$[0], [1], \ldots, [n-1]$. The set of residue classes forms a ring 
$\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$.
